policy_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to retrofit buildings to reduce building energy use, save money, and improve resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## RETROFIT BUILDINGS

Chicago's residential, commercial, and industrial buildings account for nearly 69% of the City's carbon footprint. Reducing building energy use through efficiency will reduce the city's GHG emissions, save residents and businesses money, and improve the city's resiliency to climate shocks and stressors.
mechanism_description: Physical efficiency upgrades to existing buildings reduce operational energy consumption, which lowers emissions while also providing cost savings and resilience benefits.

policy_statement: Chicago will retrofit residential buildings with 4 or fewer units by 20% by 2030 and 50% by 2040, prioritizing low- or moderate-income households.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to retrofit buildings to reduce building energy use, save money, and improve resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## Retrofit residential buildings with 4 or fewer units: 20% by 2030 and 50% by 2040, prioritizing low- or moderate-income households

By 2025, 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects will be installed within city limits, prioritizing households with the highest energy burden.
mechanism_description: Upgrading residential buildings improves efficiency and lowers the energy needed for heating, cooling, and other building operations.

policy_statement: Chicago will retrofit 20% of total industrial buildings by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to retrofit buildings to reduce building energy use, save money, and improve resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## Retrofit 20% of total industrial buildings by 2030

Industrial buildings use large amounts of energy for manufacturing and other processes, comprising 16.6% of the City's 2017 carbon footprint.
mechanism_description: Retrofitting industrial buildings improves building systems and efficiency, which lowers energy consumption and associated emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will retrofit 90% of total city-owned and sister agency-owned buildings by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to retrofit buildings to reduce building energy use, save money, and improve resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## Retrofit 90% of total city-owned and sister agency-owned buildings by 2035

City and sister agency buildings include public schools, community centers, libraries, fire and police stations, offices, and other buildings that serve the public.
mechanism_description: Retrofitting existing public buildings improves their energy performance, which reduces overall building energy use and associated emissions.

policy_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to connect communities to renewable energy through community ownership and renewable generation access.
verbatim_text:
## CONNECT COMMUNITIES TO RENEWABLE ENERGY

Expanding innovative community ownership models for renewable energy projects on City property and utilizing state funding will help Chicago achieve 100% clean renewable energy community-wide by 2035. The City will support the development of renewable energy generation to bring the economic and pollution-reduction benefits of renewable energy to Chicago homes and businesses.
mechanism_description: Supporting community-owned renewable energy projects increases clean electricity generation, which displaces fossil fuel power and lowers emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will install 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to connect communities to renewable energy through community ownership and renewable generation access.
verbatim_text:
## Install 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects by 2025

By 2025, 5 megawatts of co-owned community solar projects will be installed within city limits, prioritizing households with the highest energy burden.
mechanism_description: Installing community solar projects increases renewable electricity generation, which displaces fossil-based power on the grid.

policy_statement: Chicago will increase Chicago-based community renewables to 20 megawatts by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to connect communities to renewable energy through community ownership and renewable generation access.
verbatim_text:
## Increase Chicago-based community renewables to 20 megawatts by 2025

By 2025, the City will increase Chicago-based community renewables to 20 megawatts.
mechanism_description: Expanding community renewable energy capacity increases clean electricity supply and displaces fossil-fuel-based power generation on the grid.

policy_statement: Chicago will increase community renewables subscriptions so that 25% are subscribed by low-income and/or environmental justice low-income residents by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 1 commits Chicago to connect communities to renewable energy through community ownership and renewable generation access.
verbatim_text:
## Increase community renewables subscriptions to achieve 25% subscribed by low-income and/ or environmental justice low-income residents by 2030

By 2030, the City will increase community renewable subscriptions to achieve a 25% subscription rate by low-income and/or environmental justice low-income residents.
mechanism_description: Expanding community renewable energy subscriptions increases demand for and use of renewable electricity, helping displace fossil-based power generation.

policy_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## REDUCE WASTE AND LANDFILLING

Acknowledging that reducing overall waste provides the greatest benefits, it is also important to improve the convenience and effectiveness of waste management infrastructure, so that it is easier for individuals and businesses to divert waste from landfills.
mechanism_description: Improving waste diversion and related management practices reduces the amount of material landfilled, thereby avoiding methane emissions from landfill decomposition.

policy_statement: Chicago will introduce community-wide organic waste collection and decomposition by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Introduce community-wide organic waste collection and decomposition by 2025

In households and restaurants, food waste is typically the largest portion of the waste stream.
mechanism_description: Separating and composting organic waste diverts food scraps from landfills, reducing methane emissions while recovering useful nutrients.

policy_statement: Chicago will implement equitable waste source prevention strategies by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Implement equitable waste source prevention strategies by 2030

Eliminating waste, particularly by avoiding single-use products, brings many benefits.
mechanism_description: Preventing waste at the source, such as by avoiding single-use products, reduces the amount of material produced and discarded, lowering disposal-related emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will divert 90% of commercial, industrial, and institutional waste by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Divert 90% of commercial, industrial, and institutional waste by 2030

To achieve a 90% diversion goal by 2030, the City will help commercial and industrial businesses implement new source reduction and prevention techniques and improve reuse and recycling.
mechanism_description: Reducing and redirecting waste away from landfills through prevention, reuse, and recycling lowers landfill methane emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will divert 75% of construction and demolition waste by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Divert 75% of construction and demolition waste by 2030

By 2030, the city will divert 75% of construction and demolition waste from landfills and incineration.
mechanism_description: Diverting construction and demolition waste from disposal reduces landfill-related emissions, especially methane from decomposing waste.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable building design for disassembly and reuse by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Enable building design for disassembly and reuse by 2035

When materials are reused, they are kept out of landfills and new material manufacturing is avoided.
mechanism_description: Designing buildings for future disassembly enables material reuse, which reduces landfill disposal and avoids emissions from manufacturing new construction materials.

policy_statement: Chicago will divert 90% of residential waste by 2040.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 2 commits Chicago to reduce waste and landfilling through diversion, organics collection, and reuse-oriented building practices.
verbatim_text:
## Divert 90% of residential waste by 2040

Smaller residential buildings in Chicago are currently served by the City's Blue and Black Cart Programs for recycling and garbage.
mechanism_description: Diverting residential waste from landfills reduces methane generation from decomposing waste and lowers waste-sector emissions.

policy_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to make walking, biking, and transit viable options for all trips.
verbatim_text:
## MAKE WALKING, BIKING, OR TRANSIT VIABLE OPTIONS FOR ALL TRIPS

Every neighborhood should have streets with the necessary infrastructure to ensure safe and comfortable travel for everyone.
mechanism_description: Improving infrastructure for walking, biking, and transit shifts trips away from higher-emitting vehicle travel and reduces transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will expand high-quality and low-stress on-street bikeways and off-street trails.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to make walking, biking, and transit viable options for all trips.
verbatim_text:
## Expand high-quality and low-stress on-street bikeways and off-street trails

To continue improving community mobility and support citywide transportation emissions reductions, by the end of 2024 the City aims to add 25 more miles of protected bike lanes, 25 more miles of neighborhood greenways, and meet the CDOT Strategic Plan goal of eliminating 15 miles of gaps in the citywide bikeways network.
mechanism_description: Expanding safe and connected bikeways and trails increases cycling and shifts trips away from motor vehicles, reducing transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will increase Divvy bikes and shared micromobility trips by 30% by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to make walking, biking, and transit viable options for all trips.
verbatim_text:
## Increase Divvy bikes and shared micromobility trips 30% by 2030

By the end of 2022, the Divvy bike network will expand to serve the entire city with 16,500 bikes.
mechanism_description: Expanding shared bikes and other micromobility options enables more low-carbon trips and reduces transportation emissions by displacing higher-emitting travel modes.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable Chicagoans to walk, bike, take transit, or use shared micromobility for 45% of all trips by 2040.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to make walking, biking, and transit viable options for all trips.
verbatim_text:
## Enable Chicagoans to walk, bike, take transit, or use shared micromobility for 45% of all trips by 2040

The City will invest in the lowest carbon and highest efficiency modes of transportation to increase walking, biking, transit, and shared micro-mobility use to 45% of all trips by 2040.
mechanism_description: Investing in walking, biking, transit, and shared micromobility increases trips made by low-carbon modes, reducing transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## INCREASE TRANSIT PERFORMANCE AND ENCOURAGE EQUITABLE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT

By increasing access to reliable transit, Chicagoans can reduce the number or distance of vehicle trips and related emissions.
mechanism_description: Improving transit access and concentrating development around transit reduces private vehicle travel and associated transport emissions while shaping lower-carbon land use patterns.

policy_statement: Chicago will update land use policies to encourage sustainable development, accessibility, and street safety by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## Update land use policies to encourage sustainable development, accessibility, and street safety by 2023

Chicago can expand and strengthen this land use policy to apply across the city more equitably and to promote a diversity of housing options and mixed-use developments near transit.
mechanism_description: Updating land use policies to support mixed-use and transit-adjacent development reduces car dependence and lowers transport emissions while improving land use efficiency.

policy_statement: Chicago will expand use of commuter benefits by 2024.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## Expand use of commuter benefits by 2024

Providing incentives for workers to take transit can increase transit ridership.
mechanism_description: Commuter benefits encourage workers to choose transit over more carbon-intensive travel modes, leading to lower transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will require transportation demand management plans for new development by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## Require transportation demand management plans for new development by 2025

By requiring or incentivizing new larger development to develop TDM plans, our planning and development strategies can intentionally support sustainable transportation and local ETOD policy implementation.
mechanism_description: Requiring transportation demand management plans for new development is intended to shift trips from private cars to transit, walking, and cycling, thereby reducing transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will update citywide car and bike parking requirements by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## Update citywide car and bike parking requirements by 2025

Chicago will use this opportunity to modernize parking standards to establish a better balance between all transportation modes (including biking and micromobility options) and make the city safer for pedestrians.
mechanism_description: Reforming car and bike parking requirements encourages less car-dependent travel and greater use of biking, micromobility, and walking, which reduces transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will increase CTA ridership by 20% by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to improve transit performance and encourage equitable transit-oriented development.
verbatim_text:
## Increase CTA ridership 20% by 2030

The City is committed to prioritizing citywide clean transit and incentivizing its use.
mechanism_description: Increasing public transit use shifts travel away from higher-emitting modes and reduces transportation greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## ENABLE ZERO-EMISSION TRANSIT AND FLEETS

Shifting to electric taxis, ride hail fleet, and City vehicles will replace approximately 50,000 combustion-engine vehicles. The equitable expansion and distribution of charging stations will enable more residents to benefit from electric vehicle use.
mechanism_description: Replacing combustion-engine fleet and transit vehicles with electric vehicles lowers greenhouse gas emissions from transportation.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable electric freight loading docks at commercial and industrial buildings, addressing new buildings by 2025 and existing buildings by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## Enable electric freight loading docks at commercial and industrial buildings, addressing new buildings by 2025 and existing buildings by 2030

For new buildings, electric vehicle chargers must be considered during initial design and construction stages where implementation is easiest and most cost-effective.
mechanism_description: Installing or requiring charging-ready freight loading infrastructure at buildings makes it easier to adopt electric freight vehicles, which supports lower transport emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will support equitable electrification of ride-hail and taxi fleets by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## Support equitable electrification of ride-hail and taxi fleets by 2030

Ride-hail and taxi fleet electrification will reduce citywide emissions, reduce fuel costs for ride hail and taxi drivers, and improve air quality across the city.
mechanism_description: Electrifying ride-hail and taxi vehicles replaces fossil-fuel combustion with electric drivetrains, lowering transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable 100% electrification of delivery fleets by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## Enable 100% electrification of delivery fleets by 2035

The City will enable fleet electrification to ensure these providers and others to meet their fleet electrification targets.
mechanism_description: Electrifying delivery fleets replaces fossil-fueled vehicle travel with electric vehicles, reducing transportation greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will electrify 100% of the City's fleet by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## Electrify 100% of the City's fleet by 2035

With a fleet of more than 9,400 vehicles serviced at 13 maintenance facilities and 11 fuel sites citywide, the City has an opportunity to leverage an institutional process to alleviate inequitable pollution burdens.
mechanism_description: Replacing the city's conventional vehicles with electric vehicles reduces fossil fuel use and lowers transportation greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago aims to achieve zero-emission transit fleets across Chicagoland by 2040.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 3 commits Chicago to enable zero-emission transit and fleets.
verbatim_text:
## Achieve zero-emission transit fleets across Chicagoland by 2040

Metra and PACE both aim to operate zero-emission fleets by 2040.
mechanism_description: Transitioning transit fleets to zero-emission vehicles reduces fossil fuel combustion and lowers transportation greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to achieve 100% clean renewable energy through local, regional, and city-property projects.
verbatim_text:
## 100% CLEAN RENEWABLE ENERGY

We can make our electric grid 'cleaner' and enhance power reliability by investing in the development of new renewable energy generation sources and storage.
mechanism_description: Setting and pursuing a renewable energy goal increases renewable electricity deployment, which displaces fossil-fuel generation and lowers grid emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will aggregate 5,000 megawatts of clean renewable energy within a 250-mile radius of Chicago by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to achieve 100% clean renewable energy through local, regional, and city-property projects.
verbatim_text:
## Aggregate 5,000 megawatts of clean renewable energy within a 250-mile radius of Chicago by 2030

By 2030, Chicago will bolster its relationships with surrounding counties to promote the growth of clean renewable energy, aligned with Illinois' goal of reaching 100% clean energy statewide by 2050.
mechanism_description: Increasing the amount of renewable electricity supplied to the grid displaces fossil-based generation and lowers power-sector emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will install 30 megawatts of clean renewable energy projects on City property by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to achieve 100% clean renewable energy through local, regional, and city-property projects.
verbatim_text:
## Install 30 megawatts of clean renewable energy projects on City property by 2030

Installing solar arrays on City-owned properties will ensure that previously underutilized space can reduce energy costs, increase local job opportunities, and improve citywide energy resiliency.
mechanism_description: Installing solar energy systems on city property generates clean electricity that displaces fossil-fuel power generation.

policy_statement: Chicago will achieve 100% clean renewable energy communitywide by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to achieve 100% clean renewable energy through local, regional, and city-property projects.
verbatim_text:
## Achieve 100% clean renewable energy communitywide by 2035

By 2035, the City will work with utilities to provide 100% clean renewable energy community-wide.
mechanism_description: Setting a communitywide renewable energy target shifts utility energy supply toward clean sources, reducing emissions from grid electricity generation.

policy_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable building and personal vehicle electrification.
verbatim_text:
## ENABLE BUILDING AND PERSONAL VEHICLE ELECTRIFICATION

Electrification refers to transitioning away from direct fossil-fuel combustion in equipment and vehicles by replacing fossil-fuel-powered equipment with alternatives that run on electricity.
mechanism_description: Replacing fossil-fuel-powered building equipment and personal vehicles with electric alternatives reduces on-site fuel combustion and associated greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will enact policies that support electrified renovations and new construction by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable building and personal vehicle electrification.
verbatim_text:
## Enact policies that support electrified renovations and new construction by 2023

By 2023, the City will enable electrified renovations for all existing building types (residen -tial, commercial, and industrial) and new construction.
mechanism_description: Supporting electrified renovations and new buildings shifts building systems from fossil fuels to electricity, reducing on-site fossil fuel use and associated emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable building and personal vehicle electrification.
verbatim_text:
## Electrify 30% of total existing residential buildings by 2035

To achieve this goal, the City and its private, nonprofit, and public-sector partners will support homeowners and landlords in installing new electric heat pumps for heating, cooling, and hot water.
mechanism_description: Replacing fossil-fueled heating and water-heating equipment in existing homes with electric heat pumps reduces on-site fuel combustion and building emissions.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable 2,500 new public passenger electric vehicle charging stations by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable building and personal vehicle electrification.
verbatim_text:
## Enable 2,500 new public passenger electric vehicle charging stations by 2035

In coordination with private and public electric vehicle charger operators and building owners, the City will enable the installation of 2,500 new commercial-grade, Level 2 public passenger electric vehicle charging stations by 2035, with priority given to low- and middle-income communities.
mechanism_description: Deploying public electric vehicle charging stations makes EV use more practical and convenient, which enables greater electric vehicle adoption and associated transport emissions reductions.

policy_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to align building codes and standards with climate best practices.
verbatim_text:
## ALIGN BUILDING CODES AND STANDARDS WITH CLIMATE BEST PRACTICES

To support Chicago's transition to a low-carbon future, the City must modernize its planning, zoning, and building codes.
mechanism_description: Updating building codes and standards requires more efficient building design and construction practices, which lowers operational energy use.

policy_statement: Chicago will strengthen policies that support installation of green roofs and walls, tree planting, and other vegetative cover by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to align building codes and standards with climate best practices.
verbatim_text:
## Strengthen policies that support installation of green roofs and walls, tree planting, and other vegetative cover by 2023

By 2023, the City will enable green-roof and green-wall installations, tree planting, and other vegetative cover on both existing buildings and new construction.
mechanism_description: Expanding urban vegetation through roofs, walls, trees, and other planted cover helps cool the urban environment and increases biological carbon storage.

policy_statement: Chicago will enable net-zero-carbon construction by 2040.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to align building codes and standards with climate best practices.
verbatim_text:
## Enable net-zero-carbon construction by 2040

In alignment with Building Decarbonization Working Group report by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, the City will enable net-zero-carbon construction by 2040.
mechanism_description: Requiring or enabling net-zero-carbon standards for new buildings reduces emissions by ensuring new construction uses much less energy and cleaner building systems.

policy_statement: Strategy 4 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to decommission fossil power through a transition strategy.
verbatim_text:
## DECOMMISSION FOSSIL POWER

Making our electric grid cleaner requires more than improving access to renewable energy generation or incentivizing demand response.
mechanism_description: Retiring fossil-fuel power generation reduces carbon-intensive electricity supply and shifts the grid toward lower-emissions power.

policy_statement: Chicago will develop a fossil-fuel plants transition strategy by 2024.
parent_statement: Strategy 4 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to decommission fossil power through a transition strategy.
verbatim_text:
## Develop a fossil-fuel plants transition strategy by 2024

By 2024, the City will develop a peaker plant (power plant used only when there is a high demand for electricity) transition strategy with guidance for an equitable, safe, and inclusive process.
mechanism_description: Developing a formal transition strategy for fossil-fuel power plants builds institutional capacity to manage a planned shift away from high-emitting generation.

policy_statement: Strategy 5 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable interconnection and storage to improve reliability and resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## ENABLE INTERCONNECTION AND STORAGE

To ensure that cleaning our electric grid enhances power reliability and resiliency, Chicago will increase energy storage and distribution, and will promote better demand response during periods of peak electricity usage.
mechanism_description: Expanding energy storage capacity increases grid flexibility and reliability, enabling greater integration of clean electricity and reducing dependence on fossil-based generation.

policy_statement: Chicago will ensure 150 megawatts of energy storage by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 5 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable interconnection and storage to improve reliability and resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## Ensure 150 megawatts of energy storage by 2025

Investing in energy storage capacity is necessary to enable a full transition to renewable energy.
mechanism_description: Deploying energy storage increases grid flexibility, which helps integrate more renewable electricity and reduces reliance on fossil generation.

policy_statement: Chicago will invest in 1,000 megawatts of demand response by 2024 and 3,000 megawatts by 2035.
parent_statement: Strategy 5 under Pillar 4 commits Chicago to enable interconnection and storage to improve reliability and resiliency.
verbatim_text:
## Invest in 1,000 megawatts of demand response by 2024, and 3,000 megawatts by 2035

By achieving 1,000 megawatts of new energy demand reduction capacity by 2030, and increasing to 3,000 megawatts by 2040, Chicagoans will be better protected during extreme weather events, when electrical grids are unable to supply enough power to meet surge in demand.
mechanism_description: Deploying demand response capacity reduces or shifts electricity demand during peak periods, easing grid stress and improving power system reliability during extreme events.

policy_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to collect relevant data on energy burden, water/soil quality, and air quality to support equitable climate action.
verbatim_text:
## COLLECT RELEVANT DATA

Energy burden, water quality, soil quality, and air quality data can be used to measure the equity of climate action across Chicago neighborhoods. Data will be collected by community area, where possible, to show decision-makers where and how their decisions most affect residents.
mechanism_description: Collecting neighborhood-level environmental and equity data helps decision-makers target and improve climate actions through better planning and oversight.

policy_statement: Chicago will report energy burden by community area by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to collect relevant data on energy burden, water/soil quality, and air quality to support equitable climate action.
verbatim_text:
## Report energy burden by community area by 2023

The City will analyze energy reports and other data sources to identify where building retrofits or energy subsidies are most needed.
mechanism_description: The policy gathers and analyzes community-level energy burden data to inform where future retrofit or subsidy actions should be targeted most effectively.

policy_statement: Chicago will develop a water and soil quality measurement and mitigation strategy by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to collect relevant data on energy burden, water/soil quality, and air quality to support equitable climate action.
verbatim_text:
## Develop a water and soil quality measurement and mitigation strategy by 2023

In partnership with City-operated facilities, sister agencies, and community-based organizations, proactive measurement, public reporting, and application of water and soil quality data can inform where infrastructure improvements are needed and help prioritize investments to ensure healthy, safe, and vibrant communities.
mechanism_description: Monitoring and publicly reporting water and soil quality data helps identify risks and prioritize mitigation and infrastructure investments that strengthen community resilience.

policy_statement: Chicago will establish a robust outdoor air quality monitoring network by 2025.
parent_statement: Strategy 1 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to collect relevant data on energy burden, water/soil quality, and air quality to support equitable climate action.
verbatim_text:
## Establish a robust outdoor air quality monitoring network by 2025

In addition, a monitoring network will help identify where more action is needed to ensure all residents have access to healthy air.
mechanism_description: Establishing an air quality monitoring network generates environmental data that helps officials identify pollution hotspots and better target future protective actions.

policy_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## ENABLE DATA-DRIVEN DECISION-MAKING

Achieving equity across neighborhoods will require that City policymakers and implementers have relevant and accurate data when making decisions.
mechanism_description: Integrating relevant planning metrics into decision-making improves the city's information base and supports better climate resilience and adaptation planning.

policy_statement: Chicago will publish clean energy just transition metrics by 2022.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## Publish clean energy just transition metrics by 2022

A collaboration between diverse, local stakeholders affected by the clean-energy transition will support the optimal alignment and application of the parameters set forth in the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act (CEJA).
mechanism_description: Publishing just transition metrics creates an information and monitoring system that supports better governance, stakeholder alignment, and planning for clean energy transition implementation.

policy_statement: Chicago will integrate community resilience and climate justice criteria into department-level strategic planning and annual budget setting by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## Integrate community resilience and climate justice criteria into department-level strategic planning and annual budget setting by 2023

The City will work with stakeholders to identify key health, resilience, climate, and equity criteria to assess.
mechanism_description: Integrating resilience and climate justice criteria into departmental planning and budgeting strengthens institutional capacity to evaluate and prioritize climate-adaptive and equitable actions.

policy_statement: Chicago will develop a Heat Vulnerability Index and integrate it into planning and development, community safety, and public health planning processes beginning in 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## Develop a Heat Vulnerability Index and integrate into planning and development, community safety, and public health planning processes beginning in 2023

Climate science indicates that Chicagoland will continue to experience more days of extreme heat and increased precipitation.
mechanism_description: Developing and using a heat vulnerability index improves climate risk information, enabling better planning and public health responses to extreme heat.

policy_statement: Chicago will publish citywide and community-level quality-of-life metrics on equity and sustainability by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## Publish citywide and community-level qualityof-life metrics on equity and sustainability by 2023

The City will work with community groups and frontline leaders to identify metrics that meaningfully reflect community needs, aspirations, and well-being. These metrics will be measured and publicly reported every year.
mechanism_description: Establishing and publicly reporting sustainability and equity metrics creates a monitoring system that informs decision-making, accountability, and future climate adaptation planning.

policy_statement: Chicago will assess and optimize community-level emergency management strategies related to climate preparedness by 2024.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable data-driven decision-making by integrating equity, resilience, and quality-of-life metrics into planning.
verbatim_text:
## Assess and optimize community-level emergency management strategies related to climate preparedness by 2024

As Chicago invests in climate resiliency and adaptation, it is critical that OEMC and relevant partners intentionally consider how to prepare and service communities that will feel the pressures of a climate hazard first and longest, and that have the greatest needs for a full recovery.
mechanism_description: Improving and refining community emergency management strategies increases institutional capacity to prepare for, respond to, and recover from climate-related hazards.

policy_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable community resiliency through community-led projects and hazard mitigation integration.
verbatim_text:
## ENABLE COMMUNITY RESILIENCY

As the City strives to reduce emission levels, there must also be an effort to enable greater community resilience to withstand the impacts that are underway.
mechanism_description: Community-led resilience projects and hazard mitigation integration increase institutional and community capacity to prepare for and withstand climate impacts.

policy_statement: Chicago will resource community-led climate infrastructure projects by 2022.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable community resiliency through community-led projects and hazard mitigation integration.
verbatim_text:
## Resource community-led climate infrastructure projects by 2022

By engaging residents and local leadership in project design, development, and implementation, the City can foster deeper relationships with those experiencing the climate hazards most directly and better serve those communities.
mechanism_description: Providing resources for community-led climate infrastructure projects strengthens local leadership and community capacity to plan and implement climate responses.

policy_statement: Chicago will integrate community resiliency strategies with the City's hazard mitigation planning by 2023.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 under Pillar 5 commits Chicago to enable community resiliency through community-led projects and hazard mitigation integration.
verbatim_text:
## Integrate community resiliency strategies with the City's hazard mitigation planning by 2023

As a part of the next planning cycle, the Office of Emergency Management and Communications (OEMC) will continue to explicitly develop resiliency goals and metrics to reduce the social, economic, and environmental impacts of disasters with a particular analysis of how to foster greater community resiliency during times of crisis.
mechanism_description: Integrating community resilience strategies into hazard mitigation planning strengthens institutional capacity to prepare for, respond to, and reduce the impacts of climate-related disasters.

policy_statement: Chicago aims to reduce the city's GHG emissions by a minimum of 62% by 2040.
verbatim_text:
## GHG REDUCTION Chicago's
## TARGETS

The 2022 CAP aims to chart an equitable path to reduce Chicago's GHG emissions by a minimum of 62% by 2040.
mechanism_description: A citywide greenhouse gas reduction target creates an overarching pathway to drive emissions cuts across multiple sectors of the economy.

policy_statement: Seattle City Light is implementing two charging infrastructure pilot programs, including installation of 20 public fast charging stations and a residential charging program expected to launch in fall 2018.
verbatim_text: Seattle City Light is designing and implementing two charging infrastructure pilot programs. The programs will expand public fast charging availability and support residential charging at home. Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city. The residential program will offer customers access to inhome charging at a manageable monthly cost and is expected to launch in the fall of 2018.
mechanism_description: Installing public and residential EV charging infrastructure increases charging access and convenience, which enables greater electric vehicle adoption and associated transport emissions reductions.

policy_statement: Seattle's Green Fleet Action Plan guides efforts to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels, efficiency, and procurement standards.
verbatim_text: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
mechanism_description: Electrifying the municipal vehicle fleet, supported by cleaner fuels and procurement standards, reduces transportation-related greenhouse gas emissions.

policy_statement: Seattle adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan through Resolution 31491 with a goal of reducing energy use across City-owned buildings by 20 percent by 2020 from a 2008 baseline.
verbatim_text: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline).
mechanism_description: A municipal building energy management plan drives actions in public buildings that lower overall energy consumption.

policy_statement: Seattle will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025 for reducing energy in municipal buildings and set a target of a 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
verbatim_text: Double existing budget allocation for reducing energy in municipal buildings from 2012-2015, with a goal of reducing energy use by 40 percent. Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
mechanism_description: Increasing investment in energy improvements for municipal buildings leads to reduced building energy consumption and associated emissions.

policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will benchmark, retune, and retrofit existing buildings to reduce building-sector emissions, including large-scale benchmarking, retuning, and home retrofits by 2030.
verbatim_text:
## APPROACH 1:

## BENCHMARK, RETUNE AND RETROFIT EXISTING BUILDINGS

- Benchmark 1.3 billion square feet in communitywide buildings by 2026
- Retune 1.1 billion square feet in communitywide buildings by 2030
-   Retrofit 167,500 homes to reduce energy costs 28%, prioritizing Low- to Moderate-Income (LMI) homes by 2030
mechanism_description: A building performance policy requires existing buildings to measure performance and implement operational and upgrade improvements that reduce energy use and associated emissions.

-   Maximize participation in utility scale renewable energy progra ms

policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will build ultra-low energy buildings by reducing the energy use of new buildings and transitioning County buildings toward ultra-low energy standards.
verbatim_text:
## APPROACH 3:

## BUILD ULTRA-LOW ENERGY BUILDINGS

- Reduce energy use of new buildings 20% by 2030 below 2020 levels
- All new County buildings will be Ultra-Low Energy by 2030
- Identify steps to transition to net-zero ready code
mechanism_description: Stronger ultra-low-energy and net-zero-ready standards for new buildings reduce operational energy demand in the building sector.

policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will reduce transportation-related fuel consumption through mode shift, fleet electrification, community vehicle electrification, and emissions reductions from major transport hubs by 2030.
verbatim_text:
## APPROACH 4:

## REDUCE TRANSPORTATION-RELATED FUEL CONSUMPTION

- Shift 10% of transportation mode away from single occupant vehicles by 2030
- Electrify the County fleet: 80% of light vehicles and 50% of buses by 2030
- Transition 30% of communitywide vehicles to electric power by 2030
-   Reduce greenhouse gas emissions from Miami International Airport and PortMiami operations by 50% by 2030
mechanism_description: Shifting travel away from single-occupancy vehicles and toward lower-carbon transport options reduces fuel consumption and associated transportation emissions.

mechanism_description: Reducing single-occupant vehicle travel shifts trips to lower-emission modes such as transit, walking, cycling, or shared travel, thereby lowering transportation emissions.

policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will expand and protect green and blue spaces through canopy targets, water quality improvements, and habitat preservation.
verbatim_text:
## APPROACH 5:

## EXPAND AND PROTECT GREEN AND BLUE SPACES

-   Expand community-wide tree canopy to 30% coverage by 2030. Community Recommendation
-   Ensure that all County facilities within the UDB (per 2013 delineation) shall attain an average of  at  least  30%  canopy  coverage  and  all  County  facilities  outside  the  Urban  Development Boundary (UDB) shall attain an average of at least 50% canopy coverage by 2030
-   Reduce  pollutant  loads  to  surface  waters,  including  Biscayne  Bay,  to  facilitate  recovery  of seagrasses to historic levels. Community Recommendation
-   Double the total non-wetland acreage of natural habitat in preservation
mechanism_description: Expanding and protecting urban tree canopy increases vegetation cover, which helps cool the local environment and sequester carbon.

policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will convert waste to energy through expanded cogeneration, garbage-to-energy conversion, and improved recycling by 2030.

## APPROACH 6:

## CONVERT WASTE TO ENERGY

- 48 GWh/year of electricity from cogeneration at wastewater plants by 2030
- 50% of non-recycled garbage converted to energy by 2030
- 10% increase in recycling rates and cut non-recyclables 'contamination' in half
mechanism_description: Converting non-recycled waste into usable energy reduces landfill disposal and associated methane emissions while recovering energy that can offset conventional fossil sources.
